segments.s694-g41: چنین گفت قیصر is a typical reporting phrase: چنين (thus) comes first as a discourse adverb, گفت is the past tense verb 'said' and قیصر is the subject (verb before subject here is normal poetic word order). که introduces the reported clause; اکنون (now) is the temporal adverb at the start of that subordinate clause and سپاه (army) is the clause's subject/agent.
segments.s694-g42: فرستیم is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/optative here (hortative sense: 'let us send' / 'we should send' — used to express intention/necessity). ناچار functions adverbially ('necessarily' / 'of necessity') modifying the verb. با + noun marks accompaniment or instrument ('with'), so با پیل means 'with elephants'. وگاه is a small poetic adverb (here adverbial, 'at times' / an occasional particle); its exact force is light and somewhat idiomatic in poetry.
segments.s694-g43: سخن چند گویم = 'I will say a few words.' سخن (speech/words) is the noun, with چند following as a post-nominal quantifier 'a few / some'. گویم is 1st-person-singular present-subjunctive / present used for simple future or intention ('I will say' / 'I say'). همان به که is an idiomatic marker introducing the content of what will be said (≈ 'namely that'); گنج here is the noun 'treasure', standing ready as the object of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s694-g44: کنم is 1st-person-singular present-subjunctive meaning 'I will make / I shall make' or 'I should make' (causeive sense). خوار is a predicative/attributive adjective meaning 'despised/cheap' describing گنج (object). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'), hence مانم is 1st-person-singular present-subjunctive 'I may remain' (subjunctive after تا expresses purpose), دور is adverbial 'far', ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic form), and رنج is the noun 'suffering / hardship' (object of ز).
segments.s694-g9: بمانیم is a first-person-plural hortative/suggestion (present-subjunctive form of ماند) meaning “let us remain/let us wait.” تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “until.” سوی is a directional preposition “toward/to” and خاقان is the title “Khaghan” (the ruler). شود is third-person singular subjunctive/optative of شدن used here in a passive/neutral sense “become / be brought / be” — together سوی خاقان شود = “be brought before / reach the Khaghan.”
segments.s694-g10: چو is the temporal conjunction “when/if.” بیمار is an adjective meaning “sick/ill.” شد is the verb شد (become) — here a perfective/past sense used as a simple ‘become’ (“becomes/falls”). نزد is the preposition “in the presence of / with / to” (here indicating being placed with/under treatment). درمان is a noun meaning “remedy / treatment,” and شود (third-person subjunctive/passive of شدن) expresses that he should/ will be treated — “be given treatment.”
segments.s694-g11: ور‌ای here is the archaic conditional/contrastive particle ور (= “if”) with the vocative-like element ای attached in the surface form; it introduces a supposition. دون is an adjective/noun “low, ignoble, low-born.” که is the subordinating conjunction “that/which/that (introducing the clause). پیروزگر is an agentive/adjectival form meaning “victor / victorious.” باشد is third-person subjunctive/present of بودن used optatively/hypothetically “may be / be (if so).” اوی is an archaic emphatic third-person pronoun (“he”), repeating the subject for emphasis.
segments.s694-g12: بشاهی is a literary/formal nominal or jussive formation meaning “kingship” or roughly ‘let him be king’ in context (a wish/command that he rule). بسان is a comparative preposition “like/as.” پدر is “father” (here meaning ancestor/forebear). باشد is again the third-person subjunctive/optative “may he be / let him be.” اوی repeats the emphatic pronoun “he.” The pair of clauses expresses a wish: if the low-born man wins, let him rule and be like his father.
segments.s694-g13: همان is an emphatic demonstrative “that same / the very one.” به here functions as a directional/introductory particle tied to the verb phrase (literary usage: ‘let that one be…’). کز is a relative particle = از که “who/which/from” (introducing a relative clause). ایدر is the archaic form of این‌در / ایـدر “here.” شود is third-person subjunctive/passive of شدن used jussively “should go / be sent / set out.” با is the preposition “with,” and سپاه is “army.” Together: “that very one who (from) here should go with the army.”
segments.s694-g14: مگر here means “unless / provided that” (introducing the condition). کینه is “hatred / grudge.” در is the preposition “in.” دل is “heart” (figurative center of feeling). ندارد is the negative present/past auxiliary of داشتن “does not have / he has not.” نگاه here is a deverbal/nominal use meaning roughly “harboring/keeping (a feeling)” or “regard,” used with ندارد to mean “does not harbor/keep.” The whole clause sets the condition: “provided he has no grudge in his heart.”
segments.s694-g15: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بشنید is the past-tense third-person singular of شنیدن “he heard.” دستور is the noun “order / command” (the thing heard). دانا is the adjective “wise,” and سخن literally means “speech,” but here دانا سخن functions idiomatically as an epithet ‘the wise speaker / the sage’ (i.e., the wise man who speaks).
segments.s694-g16: به + فرمود is a literary sequence meaning “he said / he commanded” (به is a light prepositional/introductory particle used with گفت/فرمود in classical style). تا introduces purpose/result “so that / that,” and زیجهای is the plural of زیج “zij” (astronomical tables/handbooks) with the plural suffix -های. کهن is the adjective “ancient / old.” Together: “he ordered (them) to bring/consult the ancient zijs.”
segments.s694-g1: چوقیصر (‘چو قیصر’) is a temporal/agent phrase meaning ‘when (the) Caesar’ or ‘as the Caesar’. نگه کرد is a past action ‘looked’ (نگه as ‘gaze’ + کرد as the past ‘did’). و simply coordinates the two actions, and نامه بخواند is a past action ‘read the letter’ (بخواند = read). The clause order is typical of classical Persian: temporal + subject + verb, then a coordinated verb phrase.
segments.s694-g2: ز هر گونه اندیشه is a source phrase ‘from every kind of thought’ (ز = 'from', هر گونه = 'every kind', اندیشه = 'thought'). بر دل براند is a transitive clause with دل as the affected locus (‘from his heart’) and براند (راند) meaning ‘drive away’. Word order is somewhat inverted: the source phrase precedes the verb, yielding ‘he drove every sort of thought away from the heart’.
segments.s694-g3: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase ‘after that/thereupon’. بدستور (‘by order’) qualifies the manner or authority of the speech, and پرمایه is an adjective meaning ‘weighty/substantial’ describing how he spoke. گفت here denotes that he ‘spoke/said’ (classical narration uses گفت to introduce reported speech).
segments.s694-g4: که introduces the content of what was said (‘that’). این راز را is the definite object ‘this secret’ (را marks the object). بازخواه functions as a verb meaning ‘bring back/reveal’ (future/optative sense: ‘bring out’), and از نهفت is a prepositional phrase ‘from concealment’ (نهفت = hidden). Together: ‘…said that you should bring this secret out of hiding.’
segments.s694-g5: نگه کن is an imperative phrase ‘look!’ or ‘take heed!’ (نگه = look/gaze, کن = imperative ‘do’). خسرو is the vocative/name (Khosrow). بدین کار is a prepositional phrase ‘about/over this matter’ (بدین = 'in/with this', کار = 'affair/deed'), and زار here is a predicate adjective ‘wretched’; the clause means ‘Khosrow becomes/shall be wretched over this affair.’
segments.s694-g6: شود شاد is the result clause ‘becomes/ will be happy’ (شود = become, شاد = happy). اگر introduces the condition ‘if’; پیچد از روزگار is an idiom ‘if fortune turns’ (پیچد = turn/change, از روزگار = ‘of fate/fortune’). Note the poetic order: apodosis (main clause) precedes the protasis (if-clause).
segments.s694-g7: گرای دون is a nominal phrase ‘the lowly/ignoble inclination/faction’ (گرای = inclination/orientation, دون = base/low). که گویی که is idiomatic: گویی که = ‘as if / one might say that’; پیروز نیست is the negative predication ‘is not victorious’. The clause thus reports the attitude/saying of the base faction: ‘they act as if he is not victorious.’
segments.s694-g8: ازان پس is temporal ‘from that time on/thereafter’. و here functions like ‘then/so’ linking the result; را is the object/topic marker used for emphasis, نیز means ‘also/too’, and نوروز نیست literally ‘there is no Nowruz’ (i.e., no jubilation/renewal). The line states that after that moment even Nowruz (symbol of renewal/joy) was absent.
segments.s694-g17: The verb ببردند (past, 3rd pl.) appears before its noun phrase subject in poetic inverted order; it means 'they took' (past). مردان اخترشناس is the subject phrase 'the men who are astronomers/astrologers' (the occupational epithet follows the head noun).
segments.s694-g18: سخن راند is a poetic/idiomatic phrase meaning 'uttered speech' or 'discussed' where سخن (speech) is the object and راند (here used as a verb) functions to indicate 'uttered/handled (the topic)'. تا introduces a temporal clause 'until' and ماند (3rd sing. past) means 'remained/was left'; از marks the source ('of/from') and پاس here denotes a 'watch' or 'sentry-period' of the night. Word order is compact and typical of classical Persian: main verb + temporal clause giving how long.
segments.s694-g19: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally/at last' placed before the noun phrase. مرد ستاره شمر is a nominal phrase: مرد 'man' qualified by ستاره (here 'star' as part of an epithet) and شمر treated as a proper name (appositive/epithet), so the whole reads 'the man of the stars, Shamar' or 'the star-counting man, Shamar.'
segments.s694-g20: به قیصر چنین گفت is 'he said thus to the Caesar': به marks the indirect object ('to Caesar'), چنین 'thus/so', and گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd sing.). کای is the archaic vocative 'O', and تاجور is the noun being addressed ('merchant'), so the sequence places the report of speech before the vocative form used in the direct address.
segments.s694-g21: نگه کردم is a light-verb construction: نگه (a glance/looking) + کردم (1st sing. past 'I did') together meaning 'I looked at / I examined'. این زیجهای کهن is the direct object 'these ancient zijes' with کهن 'ancient' as adjective after the noun, a common adjective placement in classical Persian.
segments.s694-g22: کز introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; اختر فلاطون is a genitive phrase ('the star of Plato' / 'Plato's star' — here identifying the authority), and فگندست is a past verb 'he threw' (3rd sing.) whose object is بن 'foundation/base'. The clause therefore says that Plato 'cast/threw' (figuratively: established) a base from/for that star. The order (verb before object) reflects poetic compression.
segments.s694-g23: نه بس دیر is a negated quantitative phrase meaning 'not very long' (نه = not, بس = enough/very, دیر = long time). شاهی به خسرو رسد is 'kingship will reach Khosrow': شاهی 'kingship' is the subject, به marks the beneficiary 'to Khosrow', and رسد (3rd sing.) functions as a present/future 'will come/reach' — Persian present can indicate imminent future here.
segments.s694-g24: ز introduces source/agent 'from' (poetic z). شاهنشهی گردش نو is a noun phrase 'a new turn/cycle of imperial rule' (شاهنشهی 'imperial (rule)', گردش 'turn/cycle', نو 'new'), and رسد again is the verb 'will come/arrive'. The clause states that a fresh cycle of imperial power will occur.
segments.s694-g25: برین گونه is an older/poetic spelling of بر این گونه, meaning "in this way." تا is the temporal preposition "until" and سال بر سی وهشت literally orders the number after سال (an archaic or poetic word order) to mean "the thirty-eighth year." The whole segment = "in this way until the thirty-eighth year."
segments.s694-g26: This is the verbal/result part: گذشت is the past tense "passed." The phrase intends "the dark turn did not come," where تیره = "dark/ominous" and نیارد here functions as a negative or unexpected-form verb (not matched by the supplied senses), so the clause means roughly "without the dark turn coming, it passed." برو/گرد act as part of the verbal phrase indicating motion/turning (poetic word-order).
segments.s694-g27: چو شنید is a set phrase meaning "when (he) heard;" it is a temporal clause. قیصر is the subject "Caesar." به + دستور here is "to Dastur" (dastur functioning as a title/name), and گفت is the past tense "said." The sequence = "When Caesar heard, he said to Dastur."
segments.s694-g28: که introduces the content clause "that…" بیرون شد is a compound meaning "came out / was revealed" (بیرون = out, شد = became/happened). این آرزوی = "this wish/desire" (object), and از نهفت = "from concealment / out of hiding." Together: "that this wish had been revealed from hiding."
segments.s694-g29: چه گوییم is a deliberative/questional present/subjunctive meaning "what shall we say?" و links the two questions. این را is the object with the definite object marker را, and چه پاسخ دهیم = "what answer shall we give?" (دهیم here is the present/subjunctive/optative "we give/let us give").
segments.s694-g30: بیا is an imperative/hortative "come/let us" (here: "let us"). تا introduces purpose "so that / in order that." برین = بر این "this" (poetic spelling). رای فرخ = "fortunate/auspicious counsel/plan" (رای = counsel/plan, فرخ = fortunate). نهیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of نهاد "to place/set," used here as "let us set/adopt." The clause = "Come, let us adopt this fortunate counsel."
segments.s694-g31: گران‌مایه is an honorific adjective "precious / noble / esteemed." دستور here is the addressee/title (Dastur) and functions as the subject/name; گفت is the past tense "said/spoke," and این سخن = "these words / this speech." Word order is adjective + title + verb + object: "The noble Dastur said these words."
segments.s694-g32: که introduces the content clause "that…" در آسمان is the locative phrase "in the sky/heavens." اختر is the subject "a star," افگند is the past verb "threw/cast" and بن is the old/poetic noun "root/foundation." The clause reads literally "that in the heavens a star cast (its) foundation," i.e. the star had cast its foundation/seed (poetic cosmological image).
segments.s694-g33: This is a rhetorical question formed by an inverted, poetic word order. به introduces the sphere or respect ('in/with regard to') — here 'in manhood and learning'; مردی is an abstract/name for manliness or a notable man; و links to دانش ('and learning'). کجا functions interrogatively ('where/ever' → 'ever'), داشت is the past tense verb 'had', and کس is the indefinite 'anyone' (object/subject position shifted to the end for emphasis). Together: 'Who ever had such manhood and learning?'
segments.s694-g34: An optative wish clause. جهان داورت ('the judge/arbiter of the world') is the wished-for subject; باد is the optative particle meaning 'may (he) be'. فریاد رس is an adjectival compound 'one who reaches/responds to a cry' (literally 'cry-reacher' = 'responder to cries'). The verb 'be' (باد) applies to the whole nominal phrase: 'May the world's judge be a responder to cries.'
segments.s694-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خسرو is the proper name 'Khosrow'. سوی + مرز خاقان is a directional phrase 'toward the khagan's border'. شود is the classical third-person present/future copula/verb ('becomes/goes/arrives') used here to mark the action in the protasis: 'When Khosrow goes/turns toward the khagan's frontier...'.
segments.s694-g36: وَرا is the dative/clitic pronoun 'to him' (or 'him'). یاد with خواهد forms a semi-impersonal future: یاد خواهد = 'he/one will remember (him)' or 'he will be remembered' depending on context (classical Persian often allows this ellipsis). تن آسان شود is a passive/result clause: تن 'body' + آسان 'easy' + شود 'become' → 'his body will become easy/relieved'. The two clauses are coordinated: 'he will be remembered and his body will be eased.'
segments.s694-g37: چو (here merged with لشکر in the text) gives the temporal 'when'. ز جای دگر is a prepositional phrase 'from another place'. سازد is the present/future third-person of ساختن 'to make/raise/assemble' — here 'raises/levies' (an army). اوی is the emphatic third-person clitic 'he/it' (classical form). So: 'When he raises an army from another place...' (temporal clause).
segments.s694-g38: ز کین تو 'from/with regard to your hatred/vengeance' — کین = 'vengeance, grudge'. هرگز is 'never'. نپردازد is a negative present/future form meaning 'will not cease/escape/pay off' in classical usage; here the sense is 'will never be freed/from it' or 'will never escape (your) vengeance'. اوی again is the third-person clitic 'he'. The literal line: 'From your vengeance he will never (be) rid.'
segments.s694-g39: نگه کن is an imperative phrase 'look!' or 'observe!': نگه (root for 'look/watch') + کن (imperative 'do') = 'look!'. کنون 'now' sets the time. تو که داناتری addresses the addressee: تو = 'you', که introduces a relative/contrast 'who', داناتری is the comparative 'wiser' (داناتر + ی). The whole: 'Look now, you who are wiser...' (an address urging the wiser person to consider).
segments.s694-g40: بدین is a literary prepositional phrase 'by/with these' or 'in these (matters)'; here it links to آرزوها 'wishes/aspirations' (the object of reference). تواناتری is comparative of توانا 'able' with the second-person address implied ('you are more able/stronger'). The clause states: 'With these wishes you are more able' — i.e. 'you, being wiser, are better able regarding these hopes.'

sentences.s694-s21: Thus said Caesar: "Now we must, of necessity, send an army with elephants."
sentences.s694-s22: I will say a few words — namely that I shall make the treasure despised, so that I may remain far from suffering.
sentences.s694-s5: Let us wait until he is brought before the Khaghan; if he falls ill, let him be given treatment.
sentences.s694-s6: Or if that lowly man should be victorious, let him be king; let him be like his father.
sentences.s694-s7: Let that same man who leaves here go with the army, provided he carries no grudge in his heart.
sentences.s694-s8: When the wise man heard the order, he spoke and commanded that they fetch the ancient zij (astronomical tables).
sentences.s694-s1: When the Caesar looked and read the letter, he drove every kind of thought from his heart.
sentences.s694-s2: After that, by order he spoke in a weighty manner, saying, “Bring this secret out of hiding.”
sentences.s694-s3: Take heed: Khosrow will be wretched over this affair; he will be joyful if fortune turns.
sentences.s694-s4: The lowly faction — as if to say ‘he is not victorious’ —; from then on there was no Nowruz either.
sentences.s694-s9: The astronomers took up their work and discoursed until there remained three watches of the night.
sentences.s694-s10: At last the star-man Shamar said to Caesar: 'O merchant...'
sentences.s694-s11: I examined these ancient zij (astronomical tables) which Plato had thrown down as a foundation.
sentences.s694-s12: It will not be long before the kingship reaches Khosrow; a new turn of imperial rule will come.
sentences.s694-s13: Thus, thirty-eight years passed without a dark turn coming.
sentences.s694-s14: When Caesar heard, he said to Dastur that this wish had come out from concealment.
sentences.s694-s15: What shall we say, and what answer shall we give to this? Come, let us adopt this fortunate counsel.
sentences.s694-s16: The noble Dastur spoke these words: that in the heavens a star had cast (its) foundation.
sentences.s694-s17: Who in the world ever had such manhood and learning? May the world's judge be one who answers the cry.
sentences.s694-s18: When Khosrow turns toward the khagan's border, he will be remembered and his body will be eased.
sentences.s694-s19: When he raises an army from another place, he will never be rid of your vengeance.
sentences.s694-s20: Look now, you who are wiser — with these wishes you are more able.
